title: The Dress Elizabeth Keckley, Dressmaker

title: The Gold Nugget By Sea: Routes to California via Panama  and Cape Horn
content: sea: routes california via panama cape horn annotation though many people think migrants came california journeyed land, fact, many many migrants came sea. migrants china came boat, many east coast united states came boat around cape horn panama. 1849 map shows routes highlights gold region california. source "map gold regions california. showing routes via chagres panama, cape horn, &c.," (new-york: ensign thayer, 1849). available california historical society [http://www.library.ca.gov/goldrush/sec02.html].

title: The Gold Nugget Notes of a Voyage: Samuel Upham
content: notes voyage: samuel upham annotation "honest sam" upham one many middle-class americans traveled california strike rich gold rush. upham reached california sailing south around south america, reaching san francisco. memoirs, notes voyage california via cape horn, scenes el dorado, years 1849-'50 offer detailed accounts journey miner's life california. upham included account version popular mining song, "song gold-digger:" came quakerdelphia wash-bowl nee, i'm going california, gold-dust see. rained night day left, weather dry, sun hot froze death, o anna, don't cry! chorus— o california! that's land i'm going calaveras, wash-bowl knee. osceola board, traveled sea; every time thought home, wished wasn't brig reared like horse oats wealth— found couldn't throw thought i'd throw chorus— ann eliza! don't cry i'm gong calaveras, wash-bowl knee. thought pleasant times we'd together, dear; thought ought cry bit, couldn't find tear; pilot-bread mouth, gold-dust eye, though i'm far away, dear anna, don't cry. chorus—o ann eliza! don't cry i'm going calaveras, wash-bowl knee. soon shall mining camp, i'll look around, see gold-dust i'll pick ground. i'll scrape mountains clean, old girl, i'll drain rivers dry, pocketful rocks bring home, anna, don't cry. chorus—o california! that's land i'm going calaveras, wash-bowl knee. upham found modest success miner falling ill, 1850 left california philadelphia. thus, upham represents myriad ambitious men went west make fortunes, return east fortune proved elusive. every miner became wealthy california, many either failed outright found mining less lucrative expected. source samuel c. upham, notes voyage california, via cape horn, together scenes el dorado, years 1849-50. new york: arno press, 1973

title: The Jefferson Desk A document in process: the earliest surviving draft of Jefferson's Declaration
content: document process: earliest surviving draft jefferson's declaration annotation june 7, 1776 richard henry lee put forth resolution calling colonial independence. congress considered resolution appointed committee five men draft declaration, including thomas jefferson, john adams, benjamin franklin, roger sherman, robert livingston. june 11 june 28, jefferson produced draft shared members committee. copy "original rough draught" declaration includes changes made john adams, benjamin franklin others. declaration representatives united states america, general congress assembled. course human events becomes necessary people advance subordination hitherto remained, assume among powers earth equal independant station laws nature nature's god entitle decent respect opinions mankind requires declare causes impel change. hold truths sacred undeniable; men created equal independant, equal creation derive rights inherent inalienable, among preservation life, liberty, pursuit happiness; secure ends, governments instituted among men, deriving powers consent governed; whenever form government shall become destructive ends, right people alter abolish institute new government, laying foundation principles organising powers form, shall seem likely effect safety happiness. prudence indeed dictate governments long established changed light transient causes: accordingly experience hath shewn mankind disposed suffer evils sufferable, right abolishing forms accustomed. long train abuses usurpations, begun distinguished period, pursuing invariably object, evinces design subject arbitrary power, right, duty, throw government provide new guards future security. patient sufferance colonies; necessity constrains expunge former systems government. history present majesty, history unremitting injuries usurpations, among one fact stands single solitary contradict uniform tenor rest, direct object establishment absolute tyranny states. prove let facts submitted candid world, truth pledge faith yet unsullied falsehood. refused assent laws wholesome necessary public good: forbidden governors pass laws immediate pressing importance, unless suspended operation till assent obtained; suspended, neglected utterly attend refused pass laws accomodation large districts people unless people would relinquish right representation, right inestimable formidable tyrants alone: dissolved representative houses repeatedly continually, opposing manly firmness invasions rights people: refused long space time cause others elected, whereby legislative powers, incapable annihilation, returned people large exercise, state remaining mean time exposed dangers invasion without, convulsions within: endeavored prevent population states; purpose obstructing laws naturalization foreigners; refusing pass others encourage migrations hither; raising conditions new appropriations lands: suffered administration justice totally cease colonies, refusing assent laws establishing judiciary powers: made judges dependant alone, tenure offices, amount salaries: erected multitude new offices self-assumed power, sent hither swarms officers harrass people eat substance: kept among us times peace standing armies ships war: affected render military, independant superior civil power: combined others subject us jurisdiction foreign constitutions unacknoleged laws; giving assent pretended acts legislation, quartering large bodies armed troops among us; protecting mock-trial punishment murders commit inhabitants states; cutting trade parts world; imposing taxes us without consent; depriving us benefits trial jury; transporting us beyond seas tried pretended offences: taking away charters, altering fundamentally forms governments; suspending legislatures declaring invested power legislate us cases whatsoever: abdicated government withdrawing governors, declaring us allegiance protection: plundered seas, ravaged coasts, burnt towns destroyed lives people: time transporting large armies foreign merce naries compleat works death, desolation tyranny, already begun circumstances cruelty perfidy unworthy head civilized nation: endeavored bring inhabitants frontiers merciless indian savages, whose known rule warfare undistinguished destruction ages, sexes, conditions existence: incited treasonable insurrections fellow-subjects, allurements forfeiture confiscation property: waged cruel war human nature violating sacred rights life liberty persons distant people never offended captivating carrying slavery another hemisphere, incur miserable death transportation thither. piratical warfare, opprobrium infidel powers, warfare christian king great britain. determined keep open market men bought sold, prostituted negative suppressing every legislative attempt prohibit restrain execrable commerce: assemblage horrors might want fact distinguished die, exciting people rise arms among us, purchase liberty deprived murdering people upon also obtruded thus paying former crimes committed liberties one people, crimes urges commit lives another. every stage oppressions petitioned redress humble terms; given causal connection short-handled hoe back disabilities, petitioners contend hoe "unsafe" matter law. determination whether tool impermissibly "unsafe," however, involves simple decision whether causes injuries; heavy head traditional hammer, example, may inevitably cause many sore thumbs demonstrate hammer unsafe. noted section 6306, subdivision defines "safe" "safety" freedom danger nature employment reasonably permits" section 6306, subdivision (b) specifies determining safeguards employer must adopt, word "'safeguard' shall given broad interpretation include practicable method mitigating preventing specific danger." instant record, court cannot determine, matter law, whether statutory standards short-handled hoe safe determination properly judgment made first instance respondent agency, applying proper legal standard. sum, conclude respondent agency erred determining would "unreasonable extension authority" find short-handled hoe "unsafe hand tool" within meaning title 8, section 3316 california administrative code. source carmona, et. al. v. division industrial safety, 13 cal.3d 303 [sf. 23053. supreme court california. january 13, 1975.]

title: Evolution of the Declaration: Seneca Falls Declaration (1848)
content: evolution declaration: seneca falls declaration (1848) annotation women active abolitionist movement early 19th century often found right speak meetings participate ways denied basis sex. response, elizabeth cady stanton, lucretia mott, mary ann mcclintock, martha c. wright jane hunt proposed convention consider unequal legal, social, religious status women. 300 delegates, mostly women, gathered seneca falls, n.y. july, 1848. stanton's "declaration sentiments," based declaration independence, claimed female equality men listed "injuries usurpations" viewed grievances. "declaration" sparked broad lasting public debate rights women. course human events, becomes necessary one portion family man assume among people earth position different hitherto occupied, one laws title: Evolution of the Declaration: Abolitionist Frederick Douglass' "The Meaning of the Fourth of July for the Negro" (1852)
content: evolution declaration: abolitionist frederick douglass' meaning fourth july negro" (1852) annotation frederick douglass escaped slavery 1838 become leading writer, orator, activist behalf abolition slavery. also attended seneca falls convention 1848 supported rights women. 1850s traveled thousands miles give lectures arouse americans oppose slavery. july 5, 1852, spoke rochester, n.y., city edited abolitionist newspaper, rochester north star. oration, douglass challenged listeners reconsider meaning nation's declaration independence. fellow-citizens, pardon allow ask, called upon speak to-day? answer; day reveals days year, gross injustice cruelty constant victim. celebration sham; boasted liberty, unholy license; national greatness, swelling vanity; sounds rejoicing empty heartless; denunciation tyrants, brass fronted impudence; shouts liberty equality, hollow mockery; prayers hymns, sermons thanksgivings, religious parade solemnity, mere bombast, fraud, deception, impiety, hypocrisy--a thin veil cover crimes would disgrace nation savages. nation earth guilty practices shocking bloody people united states, hour. source frederick douglass, meaning fourth july negro" (july 5, 1852) life writings frederick douglass, volume ii pre-civil war decade 1850-1860, edited philip s. foner (new york: international publishers co., inc., 1950).

title: Evolution of the Declaration: Lincoln speaks to immigrants (1858)

title: Early Participants in the Gold Mining: Mormon Emigrants
content: early participants gold mining: mormon emigrants annotation one group emigrants participated westward expansion present sutter's fort time gold discovered mormons. one first mormon groups arrive california made members mormon battalion. battalion fought mexican-american war made way northern california way salt lake city gold rush. nine twelve laborers enlisted john sutter assist building gristmill sawmill near sutter's fort members mormon battalion one veteran mormon battalion named henry bigler recalled experiences working sutter 1886. bigler volunteered work construction sawmill james marshall fall 1847. thoughts recollections events surrounding employment winter 1847-1848 follows: earned one dollar fifty cents per day around man. hands tender soon became sore. men taken sick chills scurvy. dinner 27th september 1847], man entered quarters dressed buckskin, captain sutter wanted four men company go mountains thirty miles help build saw-mill south fork american river. sutter partners building mill hands done little work, expecting leave soon, hence wanted help. man jams wilson marshall... marshall took ox team wagon loaded provisions, tools. arrived mill site evening 29th, found several battalion boys advice levi w. hancock, stopped sutter's time passed august. surrounding country looked wild lonesome. surrounded high mountains, less covered heavy growth pine, balsam, redwood, pinion pine oak timber. place infested wolves, grizzly bears indians. arrival mill site, marshall brethren built nice double log cabin one-fourth mile mill. one part house lived family. man's name peter l. wemer [peter l. wimmer], whose wife [jennie wimmer] cook mill hands. source kenneth n owens, gold rush saints: california mormons great rush riches. norman: university oklahoma press, 2004.

title: A Personal Account: William Johnston
content: personal account: william johnston annotation william g. johnston young man never traveled far home left california. kept journal making journey california first wagon train 1849, later published 1892. originally intended small circle friends, johnston's account experiences overland trail prospector california provides rare glimpse daily life forty-niner. account dedicated messmates plains mountains, north fork, living dead: yet pursue life's journey, steps bent towards land city, streets paved gold; memory 'life's fitful fever sleep peacefully clods valley: affectionate remembrance friendship, many manly virtues, volume dedicated." selection entry johnston's diary entered california wagon party. tuesday july 24th. short way camp realized longer mountains climb; route lying foot hills western base sierra, valleys lying point fact reached california... stalwart leader, stewart, whose work bringing us may properly considered end journey, thoroughly accomplished. superior judgment never tiring energy, stood us well numberless difficulties encountered... yet never suffered disappointment abate perseverance, fruit untiring efforts, to-day realize greatness achievement, lead overland emigration memorable year, first train wagons enter california! thus contemplated mess others move reach sutter's fort, point sacramento river dispose mules wagons, account journey begun us practically reached consummation, accomplished eighty-eight days. distance, sixteen miles. total, one thousand nine hundred seventy-four. source william g. johnston, experiences forty-niner. new york: arno press, 1973.

title: Notes of a Voyage: Samuel Upham
content: notes voyage: samuel upham annotation "honest sam" upham one many middle-class americans traveled california strike rich gold rush. upham reached california sailing south around south america, reaching san francisco. memoirs, notes voyage california via cape horn, scenes el dorado, years 1849-'50 offer detailed accounts journey miner's life california. upham included account version popular mining song, "song gold-digger:" came quakerdelphia wash-bowl nee, i'm going california, gold-dust see. rained night day left, weather dry, sun hot froze death, o anna, don't cry! chorus— o california! that's land i'm going calaveras, wash-bowl knee. osceola board, traveled sea; every time thought home, wished wasn't brig reared like horse oats wealth— found couldn't throw thought i'd throw chorus— ann eliza! don't cry i'm gong calaveras, wash-bowl knee. thought pleasant times we'd together, dear; thought ought cry bit, couldn't find tear; pilot-bread mouth, gold-dust eye, though i'm far away, dear anna, don't cry. chorus—o ann eliza! don't cry i'm going calaveras, wash-bowl knee. soon shall mining camp, i'll look around, see gold-dust i'll pick ground. i'll scrape mountains clean, old girl, i'll drain rivers dry, pocketful rocks bring home, anna, don't cry. chorus—o california! that's land i'm going calaveras, wash-bowl knee. upham found modest success miner falling ill, 1850 left california philadelphia. thus, upham represents myriad ambitious men went west make fortunes, return east fortune proved elusive. every miner became wealthy california, many either failed outright found mining less lucrative expected. source samuel c. upham, notes voyage california, via cape horn, together scenes el dorado, years 1849-50. new york: arno press, 1973

title: Women and the Gold Rush: Luncena Parson's Perspective
content: women gold rush: luncena parson's perspective annotation one myth west gold rush almost exclusively mass movement men. numerous trail diaries, however, indicate presence women traveled california height gold rush. stereotype women came california unwillingly husbands prevalently accepted, begin explain motives women journeyed california alone families search adventure riches. women california prostitutes either. name positions women held, women california mined gold, ran boardinghouses laundromats, entertained theatres. one woman caught gold fever, lucena parsons, recorded journal daily activities california. excerpt journal follows: may 31. morning gold fever raged high went dig rest got little gold... came home tired night. still good spirits. june 2. went canion find bewitching ore called gold. better luck finding day, husband making 16 dollars fine dust. june 3. morning general turn mines... made 10 dollars day. june 4. went canion well, made 8 dollars day. hard work dig wash sand. source jo ann levy, saw elephant: women california gold rush. hamden: shoe string press, 1990.

title: From Sit-ins to SNCC: the Student Non-violent Coordinating Committee Statement of Purpose
content: sit-ins sncc: student non-violent coordinating committee statement purpose annotation two months following start greensboro sit-ins, black students organized fifty similar protests across south. april, students—aided veteran activist ella baker supported funding southern christian leadership conference (sclc)—created student non-violent coordinating committee (sncc) coordinate protests. founding statement indicates, embraced principles nonviolence enunciated leaders martin luther king, jr. (head sclc) embodied sit-in movement. king assumed sncc would arm sclc, start independent voice student protesters highly influential force civil rights movement. affirm philosophical religious ideal nonviolence foundation purpose, presupposition belief, manner action. nonviolence, grows judeo-christian tradition, seeks social order justice permeated love. integration human endeavor represents crucial first step towards society. nonviolence, courage displaces fear. love transcends hate. acceptance dissipates prejudice; hope ends despair. faith reconciles doubt. peace dominates war. mutual regards cancel enmity. justice overthrows injustice. redemptive community supersedes immoral social systems. appealing conscience standing moral nature human existence, nonviolence nurtures atmosphere reconciliation justice become actual possibilities. source "statement submitted student non-violent coordinating committee platform committee national democratic convention, june 7, 1960, los angeles, ca." quoted claybornee carson, struggle: sncc black awakening 1960 [1981], (cambridge, ma: harvard university press, 1995) 23-24.

title: Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters Badge: African American Activism
